text.title: Risk Management Framework
text.summary: Risk types, the four-step management process, P×I scoring with EMV, and response strategies for both threats and opportunities.

list.item: Threat — negative risk; if it occurs, it harms scope, schedule, cost, or quality.
Opportunity — positive risk; if it occurs, it improves the project outcome.
Known risk — identified and plannable (e.g., integration complexity, key-person dependency).
Unknown risk — not identifiable in advance (black swans); managed via contingency reserves and resilience, not response plans.

rule.statement: Track opportunity risks in the same register as threats, color-coded; teams that log only threats systematically under-manage upside and miss scope to exploit favorable conditions.
rule.rationale: Opportunity risks require active strategies (exploit, enhance, share) to realize; passive acceptance of positive risks leaves value on the table.

section.title: Four-Step Process
section.p: Step 1: Identify risks. Use brainstorming, lessons-learned reviews, PROMPT-list (Political, Regulatory, Organizational, Market, People, Technical), and expert interviews. Seed from project-specific artifacts: charter, ADRs, vendor SOWs — generic risks produce generic responses.

Step 2: Analyze risks. Apply qualitative P×I scoring first; escalate to quantitative EMV for risks where contingency must be defended to finance. Record one-line rationale per score citing the source artifact.
Step 3: Plan responses. Assign a strategy and response owner for every risk. Threats: Avoid / Transfer / Mitigate / Accept. Opportunities: Exploit / Enhance / Share / Accept.
Step 4: Monitor and update. Review the register weekly; verify trigger conditions; close risks with documented outcomes; conduct risk reviews at every milestone.

section.title: P×I Scoring and EMV

rule.statement: Record a one-line rationale citing the source artifact for every P and I score; scores without rationale converge to "Medium" by default and lose discrimination.
rule.rationale: Teams anchor on the middle of the scale unless forced to justify; requiring source evidence prevents the normalization of everything to "Medium" that makes the register useless for prioritization.

section.p: EMV (Expected Monetary Value) = Probability × Impact (in currency). Roll all EMVs into a single contingency line; finance only funds aggregates, not per-risk reserves.

example.note: Risk: Key developer leaves mid-project. P=0.30 (source: HR attrition data for this role), Impact=$30,000 delay cost (source: cost-estimation baseline). EMV=$9,000. Mitigation: cross-training + knowledge documentation. Residual EMV=$3,000 added to contingency.

list.item: Avoid — change the plan to eliminate the risk entirely (e.g., avoid unproven technology).
Transfer — shift the financial impact to a third party (insurance, fixed-price contracts).
Mitigate — reduce probability or impact (cross-training, redundancy, testing).
Accept (passive) — acknowledge the risk; take no proactive action.
Accept (active) — set aside contingency reserves for the risk's expected impact.
Exploit — take deliberate action to ensure the opportunity occurs.
Enhance — increase probability or impact of the opportunity.
Share — partner with another party better positioned to capture the opportunity.
Accept — take the opportunity if it presents itself without active investment.
